coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots from osmanthus fragrans as a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor for highly sensitive detection of quercetin and aluminium ion, and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Chunhe
Qin, Dongmiao
Jiang, Xiaohua
Zheng, Xiangfei
Deng, Biyang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been synthesized by simple hydrothermal heating of natural osmanthus fragrans, without any toxic ingredients or surface chemical modifications. The N-CQDs possess a high quantum yield of 21.9 %, outstanding blue fluorescence, good water dispersity, and excellent optical stability. Because the favorable inner filter effect (IFE) between N-CQDs and quercetin (QT) occurs, the addition of QT to N-CQDs can cause their fluorescence quenching. When Al3+ was added to the N-CQDs/QT system solution, it was found that the inhibition of IFE leads to the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/QT system enhancement by virtue of a specific binding of QT to aluminum ion (Al3+). Therefore, we used the N-CQDs as a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor to detect QT and Al3+. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent nanosensor can detect QT within the wide linear response in the range of 0.003−80 μmol/L with as low as 1 nmol/L detection limit. For the detection of Al3+, the N-CQDs/QT system showed linearity response toward Al3+ in a range of 0.1∼100 μmol/L and the limit of detection was found at 26 nmol/L. In addition, N-CQDs have been successfully used to efficient quantification QT in human plasma and monitor Al3+ in serum samples. Noteworthy, the N-CQDs demonstrated low toxicity toward T24 cells, which realized sensing QT and Al3+ in the living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Nanosensor
Quercetin
Aluminum ion
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CQDs to QT and Al3+
Fluorescence response to Al3+ in N-CQDs/QT system
QT and Al3+ sensing in living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765004 ) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China ( 2019GXNSFAA245076 ). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) (CMEMR2018-C18) is gratefully acknowledged.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765004) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (2019GXNSFAA245076). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) (CMEMR2018-C18) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N-CQDs synthesis, the detections of QT and Al3+, and bioimaging.
(A) Full XPS of N-CQDs, XPS high-resolution spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D) of N-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectrum of N-CQDs (5 μg/mL), Inset: the photographs of the N-CQDs solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (λex = 325-425 nm)；The concentration of N-CQDs is 5 μg/mL.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of QT in Tris−HCl buffer (50 mmol/L, pH = 7); (B) Relationship between quenching efficiency and the concentration of QT at 0.003∼180 μmol/L. Inset: a linear relationship of quenching efficiency versus the concentration of QT over the range from 0.003 to 80 μmol/L (λex = 350 nm).
Effects of coexisting organic substances (80 μmol/L) (A) and coexisting metal ions (80 μmol/L) (B) on the determination of QT (80 μmol/L).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CQDs/QT system with addition of various concentrations of Al3+ in Tris−HCl buffer (50 mmol/L, pH = 7), (B) Relationship between F'/F0' and the concentration of Al3+. Inset: a linear relationship of F'/F0' versus the concentration of Al3+ over the range from 0.1 to 100 μmol/L (λex = 350 nm). The concentration of N-CQDs is 5 μg/mL, and that of QT is 80 μmol/L.
Effects of potential interfering substances (100 μmol/L) on the determination of Al3+ (100 μmol/L).

(A) fluorescence excitation spectrum (a) and emission spectrum (b) of N-CQDs, and UV − vis absorption spectrum of QT (c); (B) Zeta-potential analysis of N-CQDs, QT, and Al3+; (C) Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs, N-CQDs + QT, and N-CQDs/QT + Al3+; (D) UV − vis absorption spectra of QT (blue curve), Al3+ (red curve), QT after the addition of Al3+ (green curve) and the theoretical spectra of QT-Al3+ (black curve: blue curve + red curve). The concentration of N-CQDs is 5 μg/mL.

Fluorescent imaging of T24 cells: T24 cells incubated with N-CQDs for 6 h (A), (A) incubated with 200 μmol/L QT for 2 h (B), and (B) incubated with 200 μmol/L Al3+ for 1 h (C). Corresponding bright field images (D, E, F).
Analytical results of QT in human plasma (n = 5).
Analytical results of Al3+ in human serum (n = 5).

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots from osmanthus fragrans as a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor for highly sensitive detection of quercetin and aluminium ion, and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been synthesized by simple hydrothermal heating of natural osmanthus fragrans, without any toxic ingredients or surface chemical modifications. The N-CQDs possess a high quantum yield of 21.9 %, outstanding blue fluorescence, good water dispersity, and excellent optical stability. Because the favorable inner filter effect (IFE) between N-CQDs and quercetin (QT) occurs, the addition of QT to N-CQDs can cause their fluorescence quenching. When Al3+ was added to the N-CQDs/QT system solution, it was found that the inhibition of IFE leads to the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/QT system enhancement by virtue of a specific binding of QT to aluminum ion (Al3+). Therefore, we used the N-CQDs as a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor to detect QT and Al3+. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent nanosensor can detect QT within the wide linear response in the range of 0.003−80 μmol/L with as low as 1 nmol/L detection limit. For the detection of Al3+, the N-CQDs/QT system showed linearity response toward Al3+ in a range of 0.1∼100 μmol/L and the limit of detection was found at 26 nmol/L. In addition, N-CQDs have been successfully used to efficient quantification QT in human plasma and monitor Al3+ in serum samples. Noteworthy, the N-CQDs demonstrated low toxicity toward T24 cells, which realized sensing QT and Al3+ in the living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin (QT) is a typical nature flavonoid derived from vegetables and fruits, leaves of plants, grains, beverages, and herbal medicines [1,2]. QT has very high physiological activities, including strong antibacterial, antitumor, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, antiallergy, antimutageny, and anti-oxidative effects. In addition, QT has been used for treatment of chronic bronchitis, coronary heart disease and hypertension [3]. A new study showed that QT can kill senescent cells and obviously extend the lives of the animal and relieve the symptoms of aging in animals [4]. Despite the high dietary intake of QT, only 0.4–1 % of QT can be excreted with urine [5]. High concentration of QT may cause headache and kidney damage, as well as reduce Glutathione S-transferase activity, resulting in DNA damage [6]. Therefore, to establish a sensitive and simple method for monitoring QT is of great significance in biochemistry, natural pharmaceutical chemistry and clinical medicine. Some reported analytical tools based on capillary electrophoresis [7], spectrophotometry [8], high performance liquid chromatography [5], electrochemical method [2], Liquid chromatography tandem mass spectroscopy [6]and fluorescence spectroscopy [9] have been utilized for the detection of QT. However, many of those methods show some severe shortages, such as high cost of instruments, poor sensitivity and selectivity. Therefore, the design of a facile, fast responding and sensitive method for QT detection is critical.

Researches on the detection of metal ions are essential for environment and human health. Aluminum is the third richest and the most prevalent metal element in the earth's crust and it is widely used in cookware, chemical industry production, sophisticated medical devices, food additives, and clinical drugs [10]. However, the superfluous intake of Al3+ can damage the central nervous system and is related to serious diseases such as osteomalacia, hemochromatosis, Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, anemia, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and dementia [11,12]. Undoubtedly, analysis of Al3+ in environment and biological systems is an important research direction. Till now, the main techniques for determining Al3+ were atomic absorption spectrometry and atomic emission spectrometry [13], high-performance liquid chromatography [14], electrochemistry [15], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [16]. However, they all have limitations caused by complex operation, time consuming, expensive instrumentation and low sensitivity. In addition, Al3+ has poor coordination and lacks spectral features because of its strong hydration, which makes it more difficult to detect compared with other metal ions [17]. Therefore, we are necessary to establish a time-saving, accurate, simple and sensitive sensing platform for detection of Al3+.
Owing to their important biological functions, it is necessary to detect QT and Al3+ in biological systems.

Fluorescence probe is considered as the most efficient strategy to detect a wide variety of chemicals due to their fast high response speed, high sensitivity and good selectivity. Fluorescence imaging technique has many advantages, such as non-separation, specific recognition, less invasion, real-time monitoring, analytical visualization, and easy operation [18–20], which makes it more suitable for the detection of analytes in living cells. Recently, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become promising candidates for super-resolution bioimaging due to their high biocompatibility and exceptionally low toxicity in vivo. Moreover, CQDs have many excellent features, such as small size, unique optical properties, extraordinary fluorescence stability, good solubility in water, tunable emission wavelength, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, near infrared photoluminescence and environmental friendliness [21,22], which makes them favorable applications in fluorescent sensing, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, biological labeling, drug delivery, and bio-imaging [23,24]. In practice, the quantum yield (QY) is an important nature of CQDs. Furthermore, the passivating surface of CQDs with appropriate functional groups can increase their QY. However, this process is complex, time-consuming and not economically affordable. In recent years, nitrogen doping has been found to be highly beneficial method for improving the QY of CQDs because of nitrogen atoms having a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons available for bonding with carbon atoms [25]. At present, researchers synthesize N-CQDs from small molecule precursors with high nitrogen content. Nevertheless, these small molecule precursors are costly and highly biotoxic. By contrast, natural biomass materials are the best precursor for synthesis of N-CQDs as they have some advantages such as ubiquitous, nontoxic, low-cost, and renewable. However, the N-CQDs prepared from low-cost, green biomass are limited by the low QY, which limits their wider application [26]. Therefore, a simple and environment friendly method for preparing N-CQDs with high QY remains highly desirable.

Considering that osmanthus fragrans is rich in nitrogen elements, by using osmanthus fragrans as precursor, we present a facile and green approach to synthesize fluorescent N-CQDs through one-step hydrothermal strategy. The addition of QT can induce quenching of the fluorescence of N-CQDs through inner filter effect (IFE). When adding Al3+ to the N-CQDs/QT system subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was recovered due to the strong binding ability of QT with Al3+. Based on the fluorescence change, we designed a novel off-on fluorescent nanosensor based N-CQDs for sensing QT and Al3+ in fluid samples. Especially, considering excellent biocompatibility of N-CQDs, it was used as an imaging probe for determination of QT and Al3+ in living cells. The schematic diagram of N-CQDs synthesis, the detection of QT and Al3+, and bioimaging application appear in Scheme 1 .

Al(NO3)3·9H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2, ZnCl2, AgCl, CdCl2, KCl, NaCl, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CoCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, HgCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were produced by Guangdong Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Dulbeco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) was received from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). quercetin, threonine (Thr), L-tyrosine (L-Tyr), arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), aspartic acid (Asp), phenyl-alanine (Phe), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), homocysteine (Hcy), valine (Val), serine (Ser), leucine (Leu), glutathione (GSH), glucose, sucrose, fructose 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical and biological reagents were analytical pure and need no further purification. Double-distilled water (DDW) has been used throughout the analysis.

The JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) was employed for investigation of the morphology and lattice of the N-CQDs. The crystal structures of N-CQDs were analyzed by a D/max 2550 VB/PC X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Japan). The UV absorption spectra and fluorescent (FL) spectra were taken on Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) and LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) respectively. All the fluorescence lifetimes were measured on a Edinburgh FLS1000 dedicated Fluorescence. PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) was used for Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy detection. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The confocal microscopy Fluoview FV1000 (Olympus, Japan) was used for fluorescence imaging.

N-CQDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of osmanthus fragrans for the first time here. Typically, 10 g of osmanthus fragrans was dispersed in 20 mL DDW. Then the combine mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h to obtain N-CQDs solution. When the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The large aggregates from the resulted suspensions were eliminated by 0.22 μm filter membranes. Subsequently, the solution containing N-CQDs was collected for dialysis using a dialysis bag (1000 Da molecular weight cut off) for 24 h. Finally, the resulting N-CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CQDs to QT and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: First, 5 μL of N-CQDs (5 μg/mL) dispersion and 100 μL of various concentrations of QT were mixed in the tube and diluted to 500 μL using 50 mmol/L Tris−HCl (pH = 7.0). After fully mixing the above substances and reacting for 2 min, the fluorescence spectra (Ex = 350 nm, Em = 435 nm) was detected. For the detection of Al3+, 100 μL of QT solution (80 μmol/L) and 5 μL of 5 μg/mL N-CQDs solution were added into tube and acted 2 min. Then, 100 μL of various concentrations of Al3+ were transferred to the above N-CQDs/QT system solution, and the final volume of the mixture was fixed at 500 μL using 50 mmol/L Tris−HCl (pH = 7.0). Then, the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm was checked after thoroughly shaking and placement for 2 min at room temperature.

To investigate practicality of the proposed method for analysis QT in real samples, the N-CQDs were used to detect QT in plasma of three healthy adult female volunteers after they took QT by oral medication. The plasma samples were obtained from the three healthy volunteers (aged 48, 45, and 40) at the Hospital of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine of Guilin (Guangxi, China). After taking 2 mL blank blood sample from the elbow vein, 2 tablets of QT (containing 2 × 20 mg QT) were delivered with 250 mL warm water. When taking the drug for 1 h, draw 2 mL of the blood sample at elbow vein. And then the samples were sent in centrifuge tube pretreated with heparin sodium. All the blood samples were extracted according to the previous report [27]. Firstly, to separate the plasma, they were centrifuged for 15 min at 3500 rpm. Subsequently, plasma of 200 μL, 50 μL of 0.10 mol/L NaOH and 1 mL ethyl acetate were added into centrifugal tube and the mixture was whirlpool-oscillated. After 5 min, the above mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm. The upper layer ethyl acetate was decanted into another centrifugal tube. Using the previous step, the bottom substrate was extracted twice again. Then, under the condition of dry nitrogen flow at 80 °C, the extracts were evaporated to the dry state, and then dispersed in 200 μL DDW. Finally, in order to accurately detect QT, we filtered the residue dissolved in the water with a 0.45 μm filter.

Human blood samples were obtained from the Hospital of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine of Guilin (Guangxi, China). The obtained serum samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the protein precipitate, and then filtered via a 0.45 μm Millipore filter. The above serum samples were spiked with different concentrations of Al3+. The final concentration of Al3+ in serum samples were tested by the above developed nanosensor.

For cell imaging studies, Human bladder cancer T24 cells were propagated in culture media (DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum) at 37 °C for 24 h and 5 % CO2. Afterward, 100 μL N-CQDs (100 μg/mL) were introduced into culture medium containing living cells, followed by further 6 h culture at 37 °C. Thereafter, the supernatant was aspirated and the cells were washed using PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) to remove any remaining N-CQDs outside of cells. Immediately, cellular fluorescence imaging was performed by a confocal fluorescence microscope. To assess the actual practicability of N-CQDs for detection of QT and Al3+ in living T24 cells, 200 μmol/L QT was introduced into the living cell system for 2 h. Subsequently, 200 μmol/L Al3+ was placed into the cells and incubated for 1 h. After that, fluorescence images were recorded using a confocal fluorescence microscope.

The synthesized N-CQDs were characterized using TEM, XRD pattern and FT-IR in order to study their morphology, crystal phase and surface groups (Fig. S1A-D). These related results and discussions are listed in the Supporting Information. To further characterize surface state of the N-CQDs, XPS was carried out (Fig. 1 ). The obtained information about the typical peaks is described in detail, which shown in the Supporting Information.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrum and fluorescence emission spectroscopy were used as powerful tools to verify the fluorescent properties of N-CQDs. As seen from Fig. 2 A, N-CQDs show a characteristic absorption band positioned at 284 nm, which is originated from π–π* electronic transition of CO bond. The fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 2A showed that the corresponding maximum emission wavelengths of N-CQDs are 435 nm when excited with 350 nm excitation wavelengths. Insets of Fig. 2A showed that the N-CQDs solution exhibited intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiating. The N-CQDs showed a typical excitation wavelength dependence behavior like other CQDs. Fig. 2B displayed that the emission peak position was red-shifted from 430 nm to 500 nm when excited at 325 − 425 nm. Although the exact mechanisms of fluorescent properties of carbon dots are awaiting verification, previous reports confirm that this excitation-dependent phenomenon may originate from the different sizes and the significant distribution of emissive trap sites of the N-CQDs [28,29]. The QY of the N-CQDs was about 21.9 % with respect to quinine sulfate according to eqn. S1 and Table S1 (Fig. S2).
The fluorescence stability of the N-CQDs and condition optimization are shown in the Supporting Information (Fig. S3A-D and Fig. S4A and B).

The sensing sensitivity of the proposed N-CQDs toward QT was investigated according to the above-mentioned optimization. Then, the fluorescence behavior of N-CQDs toward various concentrations of QT was investigated. From Fig. 3 A, upon adding QT to the N-CQDs, a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs at 435 nm occurred with an increase of the concentration of OT, indicating the feasibility of the constructed N-CQDs for QT detection. Also, when the QT concentration reaches to 80 μmol/L, the fluorescence of the N-CQDs was quenched more than 70 %, and after that, the decline trend slowed down with adding more QT. As presented in Fig. 3B, the quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) is linearly proportional to the QT concentration, where F0 and F referred to the fluorescence intensity without and with QT in N-CQDs solution, respectively. The fitted linear regression equation is (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0356 + 0.00923C, where C is the concentration of QT. The linear range of QT is from 0.003–80 μmol/L (R2 = 0.9993) with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1 nmol/L (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.003 μmol/L. The reported representative detection methods of QT are listed in Table S2. As shown in Table S2, the detection limit of QT based on our proposed fluorescent nanosensor is lower than 70 times of the reported methods. These results indicate that the sensitivity of established method is better than those reported methods.

In addition to sensitivity, specificity is an important property of a promising probe. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs existed some common biological substance, such as 16 kinds of amino acids (L-Tyr, Thr, Ala, Gly, Glu, Asp, Arg, Phe, His, Hcy, Val, Ser, Leu, AA, L-Cys, Lys), glutathione (GSH), glucose, sucrose, fructose were carried out to test the specificity of N-CQDs for QT under the same conditions. The fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs (5 μg/mL) with 80 μmol/L interferents were demonstrated in Fig. 4 A. As illustrated in Fig. 4A, N-CQDs have no response to the biological substances. On the contrary, after the introduction of QT, a remarkable decrease in fluorescence signal of N-CQDs occurred. We also evaluated potential interfering of other metal cations including Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Ni2+. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs did not change significantly upon mixing these metal cations with N-CQDs. The results clearly indicated that proposed nanosensor has excellent sensing selectivity for QT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response to Al3+ in N-CQDs/QT system
ce:section.ce:para: Because QT has a complete large π bonding conjugated system and strong coordination O atoms, it can interact with Al3+ to form a stable QT-Al3+ complex [3], which allows the N-CQDs/QT system to detect Al3+. Therefore, the emission spectra of the N-CQDs, N-CQDs + QT, N-CQDs/QT + Al3+, N-CQDs + Al3+ were examined to verify the feasibility of the N-CQDs/QT system solution detection of Al3+. As depicted in Fig. S5, the N-CQDs have a strong blue fluorescence at 350 nm excitation. When QT was introduced into N-CQDs solution, the apparent fluorescence quenching was observed. It can also be seen from Fig. S5 that the presence of Al3+ can effectively restore fluorescence of the N-CQDs/QT system. When Al3+ was added to the N-CQDs solution, the fluorescence of the N-CQDs did not undergo any change. All of these results revealed that N-CQDs/QT system has excellent detection capability of Al3+.

Based on the above analysis, we incubated different concentrations of Al3+ with the N-CQDs/QT system solution to appreciate the sensing performance of the N-CQDs/QT system. As the Al3+ concentration increased, the fluorescence of system also enhanced accordingly (Fig. 5 A). Obviously, an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9985) between the ratio F'/F0' (F0' and F' represented the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/QT system without and with Al3+, respectively) and the concentration of Al3+ can be established, which conforms the regression equation F'/F0' = 1.09 + 0.0171C (Fig. 5B). The linear range of Al3+ is from 0.1–100 μmol/L (R2 = 0.9985). The LOD of Al3+ is 26 nmol/L (signal-to-noise ratio = 3), which is lower than the LODs of previous approaches (Table S3). The LOQ of Al3+ is 0.1 μmol/L.

To assess the selectivity of Al3+, the fluorescence responses of N-CQDs to possible coexistence metal ions including Cd2+, K+, Na+, Co2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ag+ were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , only the addition of Al3+ could exhibited FL enhancement effect on N-CQDs, whereas the presence of other metal ions did not cause the fluorescence change of system solution and exhibited negligible effect on Al3+ detection. This is because the strong affinity between Al3+ and QT prevents the interaction of OT with N-CQDs, thus the fluorescence of system can be recovered, indicating the excellent Al3+ selectivity of the sensing system.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were collected to explore the mechanism of N-CQDs response to QT (Fig. 7 A). The inner filter effect (IFE) usually occurs between the absorber and fluorophore and is result from the absorption of excitation and/or emission light of the fluorophore in the detection system. A good overlap between the absorption spectra and the excitation and/or emission spectra is essential factor for the IFE to occur [30]. As shown in Fig. 7A, N-CQDs possess strong excitation ranging from 280 to 450 nm with a strong excitation center at 350 nm. The emission spectrum band of N-CQDs is from 350 to 550 nm with the maximum emission appeared at 435 nm. However, QT exhibits wider absorption band between 200 and 420 nm and shows the strong absorbance at 256 nm and 355 nm. It is shown that the excitation spectrum and partial emission spectrum of N-CQDs coincided with the absorption spectrum of QT, which implies that the excitation light and the emission light of N-CQDs is absorbed by QT. As a consequence, it is reasonable to confirm that inner filter effect (IFE) should be supposed to be mechanism of fluorescence suppression of N-CQDs. The zeta potential was also measured to further understand the mechanism. As illustrated in Fig. 7B, the zeta potential of N-CQDs aqueous solution was -6.18 mV and that of QT was -10.8 mV, indicating that no complexation could occur between QT and N-CQDs. Fluorescence lifetime is a favorable evidence to prove whether quenching is dynamic quenching or static quenching. In general, the static quenching refers to the formation of a radiation-free complex, while the dynamic quenching refers to the electron transfer process. Here, fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of QT were investigated. The results were described in Fig. 7C, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was 1.98 ns and it was 1.95 ns after adding QT. The average fluorescence lifetime was almost unchanged before and after adding QT, indicating that the fluorescence quenching was a static quenching process, which also confirmed that no electron transfer occurs. All these results demonstrated that inner filter effect and static quenching effect were responsible for the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by QT.

In order to understand the fluorescence recovery mechanism induced by Al3+, UV–vis absorption spectra of Al3+, QT, and QT-Al3+ were tested. The theoretical absorption spectrum of QT-Al3+ was obtained by the sum of absorption spectra of QT and Al3+. As depicted in Fig. 7D, the UV–vis spectrum of Al3+ shows an obvious absorption peak at 300 nm. Whereas, after adding Al3+ into QT solution, the theoretical spectra QT-Al3+ mixture solution displayed two distinct absorption peaks at 300 nm and 355 nm. Moreover, the experimental spectrum of QT-Al3+ was different from the theoretical spectrum. Comparing with theoretical spectrum of QT-Al3+, the absorption peaks of QT exhibited two new absorption peaks at 270 nm and 431 nm after adding of Al3+. These results indicated that a stable complex was formed by the combination of QT with Al3+. The zeta potential of Al3+ was +7.4 mV (Fig. 7B). QT and Al3+ have the opposite zeta potential, indicating that there is electrostatic attraction between them. After mixing Al3+ with N-CQDs/QT system solution, the average fluorescence lifetime was 1.96 ns (Fig. 7C). These results indicated that QT has a strong binding ability with Al3+, leading to the formation of the Al3+/QT complex. This resulted in the elimination of the IFE between N-CQDs and QT and recovery of the N-CQDs fluorescence.

To appraise the practicability of N-CQDs in QT and Al3+ detection, the QT concentration in human plasma taking QT by oral medication was monitored. As exhibited in Table 1 , the recoveries of QT were in the range from 98.3 %−101 % with RSDs less than 2.7 %, indicating the reliability of the sensor. In addition, the N-CQDs/QT system was employed for the detection of Al3+ in human serum. The results appear in Table 2 . The recoveries vary from 98.0 % to 103 % with RSDs less than 3.4 %, which indicates that the system has credibility and can be utilized for detection of Al3+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QT and Al3+ sensing in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The study of the biocompatibility of nanosensor is of great significance for its further biological applications, especially in the field of intracellular imaging. Therefore, the cellular cytotoxicity of N-CQDs toward human bladder cancer T24 cells was studied via a MTT test. As depicted in Fig. S6, when the level of N-CQDs was as high as 1000 μg/mL, the cell viability remained more than 90 %, indicating negligible cytotoxicity of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, the results in Fig. 8 showed that the shape of cells did not change before and after incubation with N-CQDs. This result suggested that the N-CQDs could be further utilized in imaging in T24 cells due to their good biocompatibility. Subsequently, confocal fluorescence microscope was used for imaging of QT and Al3+ in T24 immediately. As shown in Fig. 8A, an intense blue fluorescence could be observed in the T24 cells (Ex = 405 nm) after introduction of N-CQDs into cells. When T24 cells were pre-treated with N-CQDs/QT for 2 h, it showed that the fluorescence became very weak (Fig. 8B). However, when 200 μmol/L Al3+ was added into the above cells loaded with N-CQDs/QT for 1 h, the outstanding blue fluorescence was observed (Fig. 8C). As illustrated in Fig. 8D-F, the cells remained in good shape. As we expected, N-CQDs could be used to sense QT and Al3+ in living cells.

A facile, economic and environmentally friendly method was developed by using osmanthus fragrans as a precursor. The obtained N-CQDs could serve as a reversible sensing platform for highly sensitive sensing QT and Al3+. This novel off-on sensing strategy would be a favorable tool to construct dual sensing platforms for tracking QT and Al3+ in clinical diagnosis and other biomedical fields.

Chunhe Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Dongmiao Qin: Writing - review & editing. Xiaohua Jiang: Writing - review & editing. Xiangfei Zheng: Writing - review & editing. Biyang Deng: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765004) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (2019GXNSFAA245076). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) (CMEMR2018-C18) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2020.113673.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
